title: Majorana-like fermion physics: Emergence of topologically protected vortical states in graphene interacting with an electromagnetic field
abstract: Within the framework of a quasi-relativistic model of graphene that admits topologically nontrivial Majorana-like quasiparticle excitations, appearance of such vortex states in the frequency dependencies of the complex dielectric permittivity of the system subjected to an external electromagnetic field has been examined. The vortex graphene states possess topological charges (flavours ) being nonzero Zak phases. Interaction effects of Majorana-like modes have been qualitatively related to the formation of Fano resonances in the optical response. The constructed topological model of graphene may be considered as a toy model of three-flavour mass-neutrino oscillations.
summary: This paper explores Majorana-like fermion physics in graphene interacting with magnetic fields, predicting the formation of topologically protected vortical states. The key finding is that these states manifest as three distinct Fano resonances in the optical conductivity, establishing a theoretical link between topological matter and neutrino oscillation phenomenology.
main_problem: The study investigates the emergence of topologically protected vortical states in graphene when subjected to an external electromagnetic field, linking Majorana-like fermion physics to observable optical responses.
main_result: The model predicts that three distinct topological resonances appear in the optical conductivity, providing evidence for three non-trivial Majorana-like modes whose behavior mimics a toy model for neutrino oscillations.
method: Theoretical analysis involving constructing and solving coupled Hamiltonian equations for Majorana-like fermions, followed by calculating the complex dielectric permittivity and analyzing resulting Fano resonances.
model_or_system: The physical system is graphene, modeled using a quasi-relativistic framework that incorporates topological features via Majorana-like mass terms derived from coupling orbital and valley currents. The model is explicitly compared to neutrino oscillation physics.
key_observables: Fano resonances in the optical conductivity ($\sigma_{Ohm}$), non-zero Zak phases ($Q$), and the appearance of three distinct energy gaps/resonances corresponding to different topological charges (flavors).
important_parameters: External electromagnetic field strength, momentum/wavevector ($\vec{q}$), and parameters governing the Majorana mass terms ($\Sigma_{BA}\Sigma_{AB}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on treating the system as a toy model for neutrino oscillations and assumes specific symmetries (like electron-hole symmetry) to derive topological constraints.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the graphene Brillouin zone, band structures showing Landau quantization effects, and complex conductivity plots demonstrating Fano resonances corresponding to multiple Majorana modes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the quasi-relativistic Hamiltonian for Majorana fermions in an EM field, deriving coupling terms between orbital and valley currents, calculating topological invariants (Zak phases), and finally predicting observable signatures like Fano resonances that confirm the multi-flavor nature of the topological states.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter topology, the explicit analogy drawn to three-flavour neutrino oscillations provides a rich theoretical framework for understanding how fundamental particle physics concepts can manifest as measurable electronic band structure features in solid-state systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
